SVI-JWK Calendar Interpolation Analysis

Combined pack covering the validation report, supporting figures, and Python implementation.

ContentCalendar no-arbitrage analysis and interpolation test evidence
Inputs includedReport, code, and five diagnostic figures
StatusPrepared for technical review
Contents
  1. Report
  2. Figures
  3. Sanitized Python implementation

Report

================================================================================
TEST 2 REGULATORY REPORT
SVI-JWK Surface-Level Calendar Construction and Interpolation Preservation
================================================================================
Document ref: document_2

================================================================================
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
================================================================================

This test challenges the documented interpolation assumption: that linearly interpolating the ATM volatility and
other SVI-JWK parameters in the time dimension produces a valid volatility
surface at off-grid maturities.

RESULT: The assumption is INCORRECT. Linear SVI-JWK parameter interpolation
provides NO THEORETICAL GUARANTEE of calendar-spread no-arbitrage between
grid nodes. A CONCRETE COUNTEREXAMPLE is demonstrated below, consistent with
the explicit warning in the primary academic reference for this model:
Gatheral & Jacquier (2014), Quantitative Finance 14(1), §4.2.

Five distinct findings are reported, of which two require immediate remediation.

================================================================================
VALIDATION REFERENCE
================================================================================

Reference : document_2
Subject   : documented interpolation assumption:
           "An IV out of the grid is computed by linearly interpolating
            the ATM volatility and other SVI-JWK parameters in the
            time dimension."

================================================================================
TEST DESIGN
================================================================================

Maturity grid (from document_2 §5.4 calibration data):
  [0.25, 0.33, 0.5, 0.75, 1.25, 1.33, 1.75, 2.25, 2.75, 4.25, 6.25] years
  ['3M', '4M', '6M', '9M', '15M', '16M', '21M', '27M', '33M', '4.25Y', '6.25Y']

Log-moneyness range   : k ∈ [−3.0, +3.0]   (simplified: k = log(K/F), no dividends)
Intermediate maturities tested : 100 per consecutive tenor pair
Three scenarios tested:
  A) Monotone term structure — Representative Equity A representative (document_2 §5.1)
  B) Hump-shaped term structure — earnings/dividend event
  C) Explicit counterexample — raw SVI parameters with confirmed calendar arbitrage

================================================================================
PRECONDITION CHECK: CALENDAR NO-ARBITRAGE AT GRID NODES
================================================================================

SCENARIO A — Monotone term structure:
  Result: ALL 10 consecutive node pairs PASS
  Worst node forward variance: 0.005039
  Conclusion: Any off-grid results for Scenario A are SOLELY attributable
              to the production interpolation rule.

SCENARIO B — Hump-shaped term structure:
  Result: 3 of 10 consecutive node pairs FAIL
  Failed pairs:
    6M-9M               : min forward variance = -0.34798076  [CALENDAR ARBITRAGE AT NODE]
    9M-15M              : min forward variance = -1.17141023  [CALENDAR ARBITRAGE AT NODE]
    15M-16M             : min forward variance = -0.00903061  [CALENDAR ARBITRAGE AT NODE]

  This is FINDING 5 (separate from interpolation rule):
  Non-monotone wing parameter term structures — created by earnings events
  for sample single stocks (Representative Equity A, Representative Equity B, Representative Equity C) and dividend/fiscal-year humps for local-market
  stocks — create calendar-spread arbitrage at the CALIBRATED NODE LEVEL.
  The production monitoring framework (document_2 §5.4.3) already identified
  butterfly arbitrages at >5Y maturities. This finding extends the concern
  to shorter maturities under hump-shaped term structures.

SCENARIO C — Explicit counterexample:
  Surface 1 validity:  min w₁(k) = 0.416677  [VALID — non-negative]
  Surface 2 validity:  min w₂(k) = 0.520265  [VALID — non-negative]
  Node condition:      min[w₂−w₁] = +0.028660  [PASSES — no arb at nodes]

================================================================================
FINDING 1: NO THEORETICAL GUARANTEE — COUNTEREXAMPLE CONFIRMED
================================================================================

Interpolation rule tested: Linear in raw SVI parameters (the underlying
representation of SVI-JWK). Under this rule:

  Result: CALENDAR SPREAD ARBITRAGE CONFIRMED
  Worst forward variance : -0.07021890  (negative = arbitrage)
  At interpolation weight: α = 0.4515
  At log-moneyness        : k = 0.1245

  Surface 1 total variance at k=0.1245: w₁ = 0.4561
  Interpolated variance   at k=0.1245: w_α = 0.3859
  Forward variance                                      : −0.0702  [ARBITRAGE]

  This demonstrates definitively that linear SVI parameter interpolation
  does NOT guarantee calendar-spread no-arbitrage, even when:
  (a) Both surfaces are individually valid (non-negative total variance)
  (b) The node condition w₂(k) ≥ w₁(k) is satisfied for ALL k

  Published reference: Gatheral & Jacquier (2014), Quantitative Finance
  14(1), §4.2: "In general, there is no reason that a linear interpolation
  of SVI parameters between maturities should generate an arbitrage-free
  surface." This test provides the concrete counterexample confirming that
  statement for the SVI-JWK model used in document_2.

================================================================================
FINDING 2: EXACT FAILURE MECHANISM — m-SHIFT AT FINITE STRIKES
================================================================================

The failure occurs through the LINEAR INTERPOLATION OF THE LOCATION
PARAMETER m (the smile centre in log-moneyness space).

Mechanism:
  Surface 1: m₁ = 0.6907 (smile centred right of k=0), σ₁ = 0.0134 (sharp, near V-shape)
  Surface 2: m₂ = -0.3828 (smile centred left of k=0),  σ₂ = 0.4978 (wide, rounded)

  At α=0.452: m_α = 0.2060

  At strike k=0.125:
  — Surface 1 is near its sharp ATM peak (k is close to m₁=0.6907)
    and has high total variance from the narrow curvature.
  — The interpolated surface has its ATM at m_α > k, so k is in the left
    wing of the interpolated surface — lower total variance.
  — Result: w_α(k) < w₁(k).  Forward variance is negative.

This mechanism is:
  — NOT detectable from asymptotic wing slope analysis (see Finding 3)
  — NOT visible from node-level checks alone
  — Relevant whenever the smile location parameter m changes materially
    across maturities, which occurs for stocks with significant forward
    price sensitivity or basis shifts

================================================================================
FINDING 3: ASYMPTOTIC SLOPES ARE MONOTONE — PROVEN ANALYTICALLY
================================================================================

The following IS preserved under linear parameter interpolation:
  Right wing slope: b_α·(1+ρ_α) ≥ b₁·(1+ρ₁)  for all α ∈ [0,1]
  Left wing slope:  b_α·(1−ρ_α) ≥ b₁·(1−ρ₁)  for all α ∈ [0,1]

Numerical verification (Scenario C):
  Right wing slope at t₁      : 0.146560
  Min right slope off-grid    : 0.147393  [≥ t₁ value: ✓]
  Left wing slope at t₁       : 0.072040
  Min left slope off-grid     : 0.072517  [≥ t₁ value: ✓]

Analytical proof:
  Right wing slope X(α) = b_α·(1+ρ_α):
  X(α) = [(1−α)b₁+αb₂]·[(1−α)(1+ρ₁)+α(1+ρ₂)]
        = product of two linear functions of α
        = downward-opening quadratic in α when (b₂−b₁)·(ρ₂−ρ₁) < 0.
  Minimum on [0,1] is at an endpoint; since X(1)≥X(0) (node condition),
  minimum = X(0) = b₁·(1+ρ₁). QED for right wing.

  Left wing slope Y(α) = b_α·(1−ρ_α):
  Y(α) − y₁ = α·H(α) where H(α) is linear in α with:
    H(0) = b₁·(1−ρ₂) + (b₂−b₁)·(1−ρ₁) ≥ 0  [proven from node condition]
    H(1) = y₂ = b₂·(1−ρ₂) ≥ y₁ > 0          [node condition]
  Since H is linear with H(0) ≥ 0 and H(1) ≥ 0, H(α) ≥ 0 on [0,1].
  Therefore Y(α) − y₁ = α·H(α) ≥ 0 for all α ∈ [0,1]. QED for left wing.

  References: Lee (2004), Mathematical Finance 14(3), pp.469-480.
              The Roger Lee moment formula establishes that asymptotic
              slopes b·(1±ρ) are the correct measure of wing behaviour.

Limitation of this result:
  This proof covers only k → ±∞. It provides NO protection against
  calendar arbitrage at finite strikes (Finding 2). The Roger Lee check
  on calibrated grid nodes, while necessary, is insufficient to guarantee
  no-arbitrage at off-grid maturities.

================================================================================
FINDING 4: MATERIAL DEVIATION IN document_2 PRODUCTION PARAMETER RANGE
================================================================================

SCENARIO A — Monotone term structure (Representative Equity A representative):

  Off-grid calendar arbitrage violations : 0  (no outright arb)
  Max deviation: production vs reference : 151.1 bps implied vol

  Tenor pair details:
    3M-4M               : max vol dev =   39.3 bps,  n_violations = 0/100
    4M-6M               : max vol dev =   93.3 bps,  n_violations = 0/100
    6M-9M               : max vol dev =   94.9 bps,  n_violations = 0/100
    9M-15M              : max vol dev =  151.1 bps,  n_violations = 0/100
    15M-16M             : max vol dev =    2.2 bps,  n_violations = 0/100
    16M-21M             : max vol dev =   39.8 bps,  n_violations = 0/100
    21M-27M             : max vol dev =   28.9 bps,  n_violations = 0/100
    27M-33M             : max vol dev =   16.1 bps,  n_violations = 0/100
    33M-4.25Y           : max vol dev =   58.0 bps,  n_violations = 0/100
    4.25Y-6.25Y         : max vol dev =   30.7 bps,  n_violations = 0/100

  The production interpolation deviates from the reference by up to
  151 bps in implied volatility. This is REAL MISPRICING of any
  option priced at an off-grid maturity, arising solely from the
  choice of interpolation method. It is not visible from the calibrated
  grid surfaces and will not be detected by any node-level check.

================================================================================
FINDING 5: NODE-LEVEL CALENDAR ARBITRAGE FROM HUMP TERM STRUCTURES
================================================================================

SCENARIO B — Hump-shaped term structure (earnings/dividend event):

  Node-level calendar arbitrage violations : 3 pairs
  Off-grid violations                      : 300
  Max deviation: production vs reference   : 570.1 bps

  The hump in wing parameters (kappa, p, c peaking at 6M) creates
  calendar arbitrage at the GRID NODE LEVEL. The off-grid violations
  at 300/300 intermediate maturities within the failing pairs confirm
  that any option pricing between those nodes will produce arbitrage prices.

  Operational relevance:
  — sample portfolio: Representative Equity A, Representative Equity B, Representative Equity C have documented earnings-driven wing humps.
    document_2 §5.4.3 already confirms butterfly arbitrages at >5Y for these
    names. This finding shows the same mechanism occurs at shorter maturities
    under hump conditions.
  — local-market sample: March fiscal year-end creates dividend-driven wing humps for
    major local-market single stocks. The manually marked parameters (§5.18)
    may not capture the inter-tenor dynamics required to prevent this.

================================================================================
CORRECT INTERPOLATION
================================================================================

Linear interpolation in TOTAL VARIANCE space (References [3], [4]):

    w(k, τ) = (1−α)·w(k, tᵢ) + α·w(k, tᵢ₊₁)
    α = (τ − tᵢ) / (tᵢ₊₁ − tᵢ)

This is the UNIQUE interpolation that:
  (a) Guarantees calendar-spread no-arbitrage between no-arb grid nodes
  (b) Is exact at grid nodes (reduces to identity at α=0 and α=1)
  (c) Is consistent with the Bergomi forward variance framework
  (d) Is recommended by Gatheral & Jacquier (2014) specifically for
      this purpose

================================================================================
REGULATORY RECOMMENDATIONS
================================================================================

REMEDIATION REQUIRED — Priority 1:
  Replace the production interpolation rule (linear in SVI-JWK parameter
  space, per document_2 §4.3) with linear total variance interpolation:
      w(k,τ) = (1−α)·w(k,t₁) + α·w(k,t₂)
  This eliminates Finding 1 (no-arb guarantee) and Finding 4 (mispricing).

REMEDIATION REQUIRED — Priority 2:
  Replace linear σ_ATM interpolation with linear ν_t·t interpolation.
  The current specification (linear in σ_ATM) produces a non-linear ATM
  total variance w_ATM(τ) = σ_τ²·τ, which is a degree-4 polynomial in α.
  Correct specification: interpolate ν_t·t linearly.

MONITORING — Until Priority 1 is implemented:
  Run daily calendar spread checks not only at calibrated grid nodes
  (per document_2 §5.4.3) but also at a minimum of 50 off-grid intermediate
  maturities per tenor interval. This addresses Finding 4.

MONITORING — Ongoing:
  Flag all stocks where any wing parameter (p_t, c_t, κ_t) has a non-
  monotone term structure (hump-shaped). These stocks require heightened
  node-level and off-grid monitoring for calendar arbitrage (Finding 5).
  Specifically, local-market single stocks around March fiscal year-end and sample single stocks in
  the 30 trading days surrounding an earnings announcement date.

================================================================================
REFERENCES
================================================================================

[1] Gatheral, J. and Jacquier, A. (2014). "Arbitrage-free SVI volatility
    surfaces." Quantitative Finance, 14(1), pp. 59-71.
    DOI: 10.1080/14697688.2013.819986
    [§4.2 explicitly states that linear SVI parameter interpolation does
    not generate an arbitrage-free surface. This test provides the
    concrete counterexample confirming that statement.]

[2] Roper, M. (2010). "Arbitrage free implied volatility surfaces."
    Working paper, University of Sydney. SSRN: 1572408.
    [Theorem 1: dw(k,t)/dt ≥ 0 globally is necessary and sufficient
    for calendar-spread no-arbitrage. Slice-by-slice parameterisation
    does not guarantee this global condition.]

[3] Buehler, H. (2006). "Consistent variance curve models."
    Finance and Stochastics, 10(2), pp. 178-203.
    [Establishes linear total variance as the correct interpolation
    framework for variance curves.]

[4] Bergomi, L. (2015). Stochastic Volatility Modeling. Chapman & Hall.
    [Chapter 2: forward variance representation. Total variance is the
    fundamental object; parameter-space interpolation is insufficient.]

[5] Lee, R.W. (2004). "The moment formula for implied volatility at
    extreme strikes." Mathematical Finance, 14(3), pp. 469-480.
    [Asymptotic wing slopes b·(1±ρ). Used in Finding 3 to show that
    asymptotic slopes are monotone — but this does not protect against
    finite-k calendar arbitrage.]

[6] Martini, C. and Mingone, A. (2022). "No arbitrage SVI."
    SIAM Journal on Financial Mathematics, 13(1), pp. 227-261.
    [Full characterisation of the no-arbitrage SVI manifold.]

[7] de Marco, S. and Martini, C. (2009). "The term structure of implied
    volatility in symmetric models with applications to Heston."
    Applied Mathematical Finance, 16(6), pp. 479-504.
    [Term structure and the necessity of working in variance space.]

================================================================================
END OF TEST 2 REPORT
================================================================================
